title: How John Coltrane Introduced the World to His Radical Sound with His Recording of ÔÇ£My Favorite ThingsÔÇØ (1961)
description: John Coltrane released ÔÇ£more sig┬¡nif┬¡i┬¡cant worksÔÇØ than his 1960 ÔÇ£My Favorite Things,ÔÇØ says Robin Wash┬¡ing┬¡ton in a PRX doc┬¡u┬¡men┬¡tary on the
body:
John Coltrane released ÔÇ£more sig┬¡nif┬¡i┬¡cant worksÔÇØ than his 1960 ÔÇ£My Favorite Things,ÔÇØ says Robin Wash┬¡ing┬¡ton in a PRX doc┬¡u┬¡men┬¡tary on the clas┬¡sic rework┬¡ing of Rodgers and HammersteinÔÇÖs Broad┬¡way hit. ÔÇ£A Love SupremeÔÇØ is often cit┬¡ed as the zenith of the saxophonistÔÇÖs career. ÔÇ£But if you tried to explain that song to an aver┬¡age lis┬¡ten┬¡er, you would lose them. [ÔÇ£My Favorite ThingsÔÇØ] is a defin┬¡i┬¡tive work that every┬¡one knows, and any┬¡one can lis┬¡ten to, and the fas┬¡ci┬¡nat┬¡ing sto┬¡ry of its evo┬¡lu┬¡tion is some┬¡thing every┬¡one can share and enjoy.ÔÇØ The song is acces┬¡si┬¡ble, a com┬¡mer┬¡cial┬¡ly suc┬¡cess┬¡ful hit, and it is also an exper┬¡i┬¡men┬¡tal mas┬¡ter┬¡piece.
Indeed, ÔÇ£My Favorite ThingsÔÇØ may be the per┬¡fect intro┬¡duc┬¡tion to ColtraneÔÇÖs exper┬¡i┬¡men┬¡tal┬¡ism. After the dizzy┬¡ing chord changes of 1959ÔÇÖs ÔÇ£Giant Steps,ÔÇØ this 14-minute, two-chord excur┬¡sion pat┬¡terned on the ragas of Ravi Shankar announced ColtraneÔÇÖs move into the modal forms he refined until his death in 1967, as well as his embrace of the sopra┬¡no sax┬¡o┬¡phone and his new quar┬¡tet. It became ÔÇ£ColtraneÔÇÖs most request┬¡ed tune,ÔÇØ says Ed Wheel┬¡er in The World Accord┬¡ing to John Coltrane, ÔÇ£and a bridge to a broad pub┬¡lic audi┬¡ence.ÔÇØ
ColtraneÔÇÖs take is also mes┬¡mer┬¡iz┬¡ing, trance-induc┬¡ing, ÔÇ£often com┬¡pared to a whirling dervish,ÔÇØ notes the Poly┬¡phon┬¡ic video above, a ref┬¡er┬¡ence to the Sufi med┬¡i┬¡ta┬¡tion tech┬¡nique of spin┬¡ning in a cir┬¡cle. ItÔÇÖs an unlike┬¡ly song choice for the exer┬¡cise, which makes it all the more fas┬¡ci┬¡nat┬¡ing. The Sound of Music, Rodgers and HammersteinÔÇÖs final Broad┬¡way col┬¡lab┬¡o┬¡ra┬¡tion, was an ÔÇ£instant clas┬¡sic,ÔÇØ and every┬¡one whoÔÇÖd seen it walked away hum┬¡ming the tune to ÔÇ£My Favorite Things.ÔÇØ By 1960, it had become a stan┬¡dard, with sev┬¡er┬¡al cov┬¡er ver┬¡sions released by Leslie Uggams, The Pete King Chorale, the Hi-LoÔÇÖs, and the Nor┬¡man Luboff Choir.


	

Hun┬¡dreds more cov┬¡ers would fol┬¡low. None of them sound┬¡ed like ColtraneÔÇÖs. The modal formÔÇöin which musi┬¡cians impro┬¡vise in dif┬¡fer┬¡ent kinds of scales over sim┬¡pli┬¡fied chord structuresÔÇöcreated the ÔÇ£open free┬¡domÔÇØ in music explored on Miles DavisÔÇÖ path┬¡break┬¡ing Kind of Blue, on which Coltrane played tenor sax. (It was Davis who bought Coltrane his first sopra┬¡no sax that year.) ColtraneÔÇÖs use of modal form in adap┬¡ta┬¡tions of pop┬¡u┬¡lar stan┬¡dards like ÔÇ£My Favorite ThingsÔÇØ and George GershwinÔÇÖs ÔÇ£Sum┬¡mer┬¡timeÔÇØ from Por┬¡gy and Bess┬áwas an explic┬¡it strat┬¡e┬¡gy to court a wider pub┬¡lic, using the famil┬¡iar to ori┬¡ent his lis┬¡ten┬¡ers to the new.
The video essay brings in the exper┬¡tise of musi┬¡cian, com┬¡pos┬¡er, and YouTu┬¡ber Adam Neely, who explains what makes Rogers and HammersteinÔÇÖs clas┬¡sic unique among show tunes, and why it appealed to Coltrane as the cen┬¡ter┬¡piece of the 1961 album of the same name. The songÔÇÖs unusu┬¡al form and struc┬¡ture allow the same melody to be played over both major and minor chords. ColtraneÔÇÖs mod┬¡i┬¡fi┬¡ca┬¡tion of the song reduces it to the two ton┬¡ics, E major and E minor, over which he and the band solo, intro┬¡duc┬¡ing a shift┬¡ing tonal┬¡i┬¡ty and mood to the melody with each chord change.


	

Neely goes into greater depth, but itÔÇÖs over┬¡all an acces┬¡si┬¡ble expla┬¡na┬¡tion of ColtraneÔÇÖs very acces┬¡si┬¡ble, yet ver┬¡tig┬¡i┬¡nous┬¡ly deep, ÔÇ£My Favorite Things.ÔÇØ Maybe only one ques┬¡tion remains. ColtraneÔÇÖs ren┬¡di┬¡tion came out four years before Julie AndrewsÔÇÖ icon┬¡ic per┬¡for┬¡mance in the film adap┬¡ta┬¡tion of The Sound of Music, evok┬¡ing the obvi┬¡ous ques┬¡tion,ÔÇØ says Wash┬¡ing┬¡ton: ÔÇ£Did he influ┬¡ence her?ÔÇØ
If you would like to sup┬¡port the mis┬¡sion of Open Cul┬¡ture, con┬¡sid┬¡er mak┬¡ing┬áa dona┬¡tion to our site. ItÔÇÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬¡tri┬¡bu┬¡tions will help us con┬¡tin┬¡ue pro┬¡vid┬¡ing the best free cul┬¡tur┬¡al and edu┬¡ca┬¡tion┬¡al mate┬¡ri┬¡als to learn┬¡ers every┬¡where. You can con┬¡tribute through Pay┬¡Pal, Patre┬¡on, and Ven┬¡mo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬¡ed Con┬¡tent:
Jazz Decon┬¡struct┬¡ed: What Makes John ColtraneÔÇÖs ÔÇ£Giant StepsÔÇØ So Ground┬¡break┬¡ing and Rad┬¡i┬¡cal?
John Coltrane Draws a Pic┬¡ture Illus┬¡trat┬¡ing the Math┬¡e┬¡mat┬¡ics of Music
Dis┬¡cov┬¡er the Church of St. John Coltrane, Found┬¡ed on the Divine Music of┬áA Love Supreme
Behold John ColtraneÔÇÖs Hand┬¡writ┬¡ten Out┬¡line for His Mas┬¡ter┬¡piece┬áA Love Supreme
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬¡cian based in Durham, NC.┬á

It took near┬¡ly 50 years. WKRP in Cincin┬¡nati is no longer just a TV sit┬¡com. It
body:
in Music, Television |   	May 11th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

It took near┬¡ly 50 years. WKRP in Cincin┬¡nati is no longer just a TV sit┬¡com. ItÔÇÖs now a real radio sta┬¡tion in Cincin┬¡nati.
A Cin┬¡cy-area FM sta┬¡tion, known as ÔÇ£The Oasis,ÔÇØ has adopt┬¡ed the WKRP call let┬¡ters after acquir┬¡ing them from a non┬¡prof┬¡it radio sta┬¡tion in North Car┬¡oli┬¡na. The Raleigh-based sta┬¡tion put the call let┬¡ters up for auc┬¡tion as part of a fundrais┬¡ing effort. And then The Oasis snapped them up.
To mark the offi┬¡cial launch last week, the sta┬¡tion played the TV showÔÇÖs theme song for six straight hours. Mov┬¡ing for┬¡ward, the sta┬¡tion will con┬¡tin┬¡ue play┬¡ing clas┬¡sic rock from the ÔÇÖ60s through the ÔÇÖ80s ÔÇö much like the music fea┬¡tured on the 1978ÔÇô82 sit┬¡com. As a bonus, Gary Sandy, who played pro┬¡gram direc┬¡tor Andy Travis, has record┬¡ed pro┬¡mos for the revived WKRP. If the orig┬¡i┬¡nal show was before your time, you can watch some episodes on YouTube. EnjoyÔÇª
If you would like to sup┬¡port the mis┬¡sion of Open Cul┬¡ture, con┬¡sid┬¡er mak┬¡ing┬áa dona┬¡tion to our site. title: Why The Founding Fathers Were Obsessed with This Muslim Ruler
description:
in History |   	May 11th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The writ┬¡ings of the Found┬¡ing Fathers of the Unit┬¡ed States of Amer┬¡i┬¡ca include many a r
body:
in History |   	May 11th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The writ┬¡ings of the Found┬¡ing Fathers of the Unit┬¡ed States of Amer┬¡i┬¡ca include many a ref┬¡er┬¡ence to the likes of Cicero, Mon┬¡tesquieu, and John Locke. That the names Hyder Ali and his son Tipu Sul┬¡tan nev┬¡er appear may not sound like much of a sur┬¡prise, even if you hap┬¡pen to know that they ruled the Indi┬¡an region of Mysore, now offi┬¡cial┬¡ly called Mysu┬¡ru, at the time. But his┬¡to┬¡ry records that more than a few Amer┬¡i┬¡cans, includ┬¡ing Thomas Jef┬¡fer┬¡son and John Adams, fol┬¡lowed with great inter┬¡est the strug┬¡gles of that father and son against the British. Those strug┬¡gles took place from the mid-eigh┬¡teenth to the ear┬¡ly nine┬¡teenth cen┬¡tu┬¡ry ÔÇö a time when the Amer┬¡i┬¡can colonies, of course, had their own con┬¡flict brew┬¡ing with the moth┬¡er┬¡land.
Hyder became the Sul┬¡tan of Mysore in the sev┬¡en┬¡teen-six┬¡ties: ÔÇ£a dan┬¡ger┬¡ous time to come to pow┬¡er in South Asia,ÔÇØ writes┬áBlake Smith at Aeon, giv┬¡en that ÔÇ£the British East India Com┬¡pa┬¡ny was expand┬¡ing its pow┬¡er through┬¡out the Sub┬¡con┬¡ti┬¡nent.ÔÇØ Ally┬¡ing with France, much like the rebelling Amer┬¡i┬¡can colonists, Hyder ÔÇ£held off the British advance for anoth┬¡er two decades, dying in 1782, just a year before the US tri┬¡umphed in its own rebel┬¡lion against Britain.ÔÇØ
Amer┬¡i┬¡caÔÇÖs fas┬¡ci┬¡na┬¡tion with Hyder and his suc┬¡ces┬¡sor Tipu, who died in bat┬¡tle with the East India Com┬¡pa┬¡ny in 1799, remained for some time. ÔÇ£MysoreÔÇÖs rulers became famil┬¡iar ref┬¡er┬¡ences in Amer┬¡i┬¡can news┬¡pa┬¡pers, poems and every┬¡day con┬¡ver┬¡sa┬¡tion. Yet, with┬¡in a gen┬¡er┬¡a┬¡tion, Amer┬¡i┬¡cans lost their sense of sol┬¡i┬¡dar┬¡i┬¡ty with the Indi┬¡an Sub┬¡con┬¡ti┬¡nent.ÔÇØ
You can learn more about this episode of his┬¡to┬¡ry from the PBS Ori┬¡gins video above. It gets into detail about the life of Tipu, known as ÔÇ£the Tiger of Mysore,ÔÇØ a nick┬¡name the man him┬¡self did much to jus┬¡ti┬¡fy. He even ÔÇ£com┬¡mis┬¡sioned a near┬¡ly life-sized automa┬¡ton of a tiger eat┬¡ing a British sol┬¡dier,ÔÇØ says the videoÔÇÖs host, which ÔÇ£includ┬¡ed a crank attached to a mech┬¡a┬¡nism inside the tigerÔÇÖs body that simul┬¡ta┬¡ne┬¡ous┬¡ly lift┬¡ed the dying manÔÇÖs arm and pro┬¡duced nois┬¡es imi┬¡tat┬¡ing his final cries.ÔÇØ Though he and his army con┬¡tin┬¡ued to fight in that spir┬¡it, MysoreÔÇÖs sit┬¡u┬¡a┬¡tion became unten┬¡able after both the U.S. and France made their peace with Britain. Despite the recen┬¡cy of the hos┬¡til┬¡i┬¡ties, the new lib┬¡er┬¡at┬¡ed colony soon became some┬¡thing of an ally in the main┬¡te┬¡nance of the British EmpireÔÇÖs remain┬¡ing ter┬¡ri┬¡to┬¡ries, India includ┬¡ed ÔÇö and would ulti┬¡mate┬¡ly learn a les┬¡son or two of its own about the glob┬¡al exten┬¡sion of pow┬¡er.

HeÔÇÖs the author of the newslet┬¡ter┬áBooks on Cities┬áas well as the books┬áÝò£ÛÁ¡ ýÜöýò¢ Û©êýºÇ┬á(No Sum┬¡ma┬¡riz┬¡ing Korea) and┬áKore┬¡an Newtro.┬áFol┬¡low him on the social net┬¡work for┬¡mer┬¡ly known as Twit┬¡ter at┬á@colinmarshall.

title: When the Nobel Prize Committee Rejected The Lord of the Rings: Tolkien ÔÇ£Has Not Measured Up to Storytelling of the Highest QualityÔÇØ (1961)
description: When J.R.R. TolkienÔÇÖs┬áLord of the Rings┬ábooks appeared in the mid-1950s, they were met with┬ávery┬ámixed reviews, an unsur┬¡pris┬¡ing recep┬¡tion giv┬¡
body:
When J.R.R. TolkienÔÇÖs┬áLord of the Rings┬ábooks appeared in the mid-1950s, they were met with┬ávery┬ámixed reviews, an unsur┬¡pris┬¡ing recep┬¡tion giv┬¡en that noth┬¡ing like them had been writ┬¡ten for adult read┬¡ers since Edmund SpenserÔÇÖs epic 16th cen┬¡tu┬¡ry Eng┬¡lish poem┬áThe Faerie Queene, per┬¡haps. At least, this was the con┬¡tention of review┬¡er Richard Hugh┬¡es, who went on to write that ÔÇ£for width of imag┬¡i┬¡na┬¡tion,ÔÇØ┬áThe Lord of the Rings┬áÔÇ£almost beg┬¡gars par┬¡al┬¡lel.ÔÇØ
Scot┬¡tish writer Nao┬¡mi Mitchi┬¡son did find a com┬¡par┬¡i┬¡son: to Sir Thomas Mal┬¡o┬¡ry, author of the 15th cen┬¡tu┬¡ry┬áLe Morte dÔÇÖArthur ÔÇö hard┬¡ly mis┬¡placed, giv┬¡en TolkienÔÇÖs day job as an Oxford don of Eng┬¡lish lit┬¡er┬¡a┬¡ture, but not the sort of thing that passed for con┬¡tem┬¡po┬¡rary writ┬¡ing in the 1950s, notwith┬¡stand┬¡ing the seri┬¡ous appre┬¡ci┬¡a┬¡tion of writ┬¡ers like W.H. Auden for TolkienÔÇÖs tril┬¡o┬¡gy. ÔÇ£No pre┬¡vi┬¡ous writer,ÔÇØ the poet remarked in a┬áNew York Times review, ÔÇ£has, to my knowl┬¡edge, cre┬¡at┬¡ed an imag┬¡i┬¡nary world and a feigned his┬¡to┬¡ry in such detail.ÔÇØ
Auden did find fault with TolkienÔÇÖs poet┬¡ry, a fact upon which crit┬¡ic Edmund Wil┬¡son seized in his scathing 1956 Lord of the Rings┬áreview. ÔÇ£Mr. Auden is appar┬¡ent┬¡ly quite insen┬¡si┬¡tive ÔÇö through lack of inter┬¡est in the oth┬¡er depart┬¡ment,ÔÇØ wrote Wil┬¡son, ÔÇ£to the fact that TolkienÔÇÖs prose is just as bad. Prose and verse are on the same lev┬¡el of pro┬¡fes┬¡so┬¡r┬¡i┬¡al ama┬¡teur┬¡ish┬¡ness.ÔÇØ Five years lat┬¡er, the Nobel prize jury would make the same judge┬¡ment when they exclud┬¡ed TolkienÔÇÖs books from con┬¡sid┬¡er┬¡a┬¡tion. TolkienÔÇÖs prose, wrote jury mem┬¡ber Anders ├ûster┬¡ling, ÔÇ£has not in any way mea┬¡sured up to sto┬¡ry┬¡telling of the high┬¡est qual┬¡i┬¡ty.ÔÇØ
The note was dis┬¡cov┬¡ered recent┬¡ly by Swedish jour┬¡nal┬¡ist Andreas Ekstr├Âm, who delved into the Nobel archive for 1961 and found that ÔÇ£the jury passed over names includ┬¡ing Lawrence Dur┬¡rell, Robert Frost, Gra┬¡ham Greene, E.M. Forster, and Tolkien to come up with their even┬¡tu┬¡al win┬¡ner, Yugosla┬¡vian writer Ivo Andri─ç,ÔÇØ as Ali┬¡son Flood reports at┬áThe Guardian. (The Nobel archives are sealed until 50 years after the year the award is giv┬¡en.) Ekstr├Âm has been read┬¡ing through the archives ÔÇ£for the past five years or so,ÔÇØ he says, ÔÇ£and this was the first time I have seen TolkienÔÇÖs name among the sug┬¡gest┬¡ed can┬¡di┬¡dates.ÔÇØ His name appeared on the list chiefly through the machi┬¡na┬¡tions of his clos┬¡est friend and chief sup┬¡port┬¡er, C.S. Lewis.
Lewis, ÔÇ£also of Oxford,ÔÇØ Wil┬¡son sneered, ÔÇ£is able to top them allÔÇØ in praise of TolkienÔÇÖs books. From the first appear┬¡ance of his Mid┬¡dle Earth fan┬¡ta┬¡sy in┬áThe Hob┬¡bit,┬áLewis promised to ÔÇ£do all in my pow┬¡er to secure for TolkienÔÇÖs great book the recog┬¡ni┬¡tion it deserves,ÔÇØ as he wrote in a 1953 let┬¡ter to British pub┬¡lish┬¡er Stan┬¡ley Unwin. In what might be con┬¡sid┬¡ered an uneth┬¡i┬¡cal pro┬¡mo┬¡tion of his friendÔÇÖs work today, Lewis respond┬¡ed tire┬¡less┬¡ly to crit┬¡ics of the tril┬¡o┬¡gy, going so far, after the pub┬¡li┬¡ca┬¡tion of┬áThe Two Tow┬¡ers, to pen an essay on the sub┬¡ject titled ÔÇ£The Dethrone┬¡ment of Pow┬¡er.ÔÇØ Here, Lewis explains the pro┬¡lix qual┬¡i┬¡ty of TolkienÔÇÖs prose ÔÇö that which crit┬¡ics called ÔÇ£tediousÔÇØ ÔÇö as a nar┬¡ra┬¡tive neces┬¡si┬¡ty: ÔÇ£I do not think he could have done it any oth┬¡er way.ÔÇØ
TolkienÔÇÖs biggest fan also urged read┬¡ers to spend more time with the books and promised that the rewards would be great. In defense of the sec┬¡ond work of the tril┬¡o┬¡gy, he con┬¡clud┬¡ed, ÔÇ£the book is too orig┬¡i┬¡nal and too opu┬¡lent for any final judg┬¡ment on a first read┬¡ing. But we know at once that it has done things to us. We are not quite the same men. And though we must ration our┬¡selves in our reread┬¡ings, I have lit┬¡tle doubt that the book will soon take its place among the indis┬¡pens┬¡ables.ÔÇØ And so has all of TolkienÔÇÖs work, becom┬¡ing the lit┬¡er┬¡ary stan┬¡dard by which high fan┬¡ta┬¡sy is mea┬¡sured, with or with┬¡out a Nobel prize.
Note: An ear┬¡li┬¡er ver┬¡sion of this post appeared on our site in 2021.

Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬¡cian based in Durham, NC.┬á

title: Bespoke Glass Studio's Sculptures Challenge Traditional Conventions of Stained Glass
description: With a stained glass window, light filters through to illuminate narrative scenes or geometric patterns, but itÔÇÖs primarily the window itself that
body:
With a stained glass window, light filters through to illuminate narrative scenes or geometric patterns, but itÔÇÖs primarily the window itself that draws our attention. For Lesley Green of Bespoke Glass, these vibrant compositions certainly arenÔÇÖt limited to these traditional apertures. ÔÇ£One of my personal obsessions is trying to convince people to hang glass on the wall instead of in the window, so you can really experience the pure color and texture of the glass,ÔÇØ she tells Colossal.
Bespoke Glass creates a wide range of aesthetic and functional forms, conceived for both residential and commercial interiors. Some are designed to be screens or separators, such as behind a bar or between tables in a restaurant. Others are more sculptural, such as her three-dimensional sculptures that project onto the wall when the sun shines through them, interacting with local shadows. This display method also highlights the inherent textures of the glass itself, from waves to ridges to mottled patterns.

Using a hand-cut, traditional copper foil method of creating the stained glass, Green has also innovated some studio-developed techniques to produce three-dimensional objects. ÔÇ£Craftsmanship is extremely important to me as well,ÔÇØ she says. ÔÇ£Precise cuts and especially smooth solder lines are part of everything my studio produces.ÔÇØ
All pieces are available to be commissioned in custom colorways, and Green is also working on larger-scale versions of the sculptural works, plus multiple-piece collections designed to installed on the wall. See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Bespoke Glass Studio's Sculptures Challenge Traditional Conventions of Stained Glass

title: Creative Thought Is Essential: A Letter from Our Editor
description:
Dear readers,
In a collection of her published diary entries, Virgina Woolf wrote, ÔÇ£Thinking is my fighting.ÔÇØ This sentiment was a prominent theme
body:
Dear readers,
In a collection of her published diary entries, Virgina Woolf wrote, ÔÇ£Thinking is my fighting.ÔÇØ This sentiment was a prominent theme in her 1940 essay, Thoughts on Peace In an Air Raid, in which the British writer framed the importance of individual thought as a crucial antidote to war and the rise of fascism.
IÔÇÖve been thinking a lot about WoolfÔÇÖs quote and its relevance today, largely connected to discourse on education. While literacy rates in the U.S. continue to plummet, a soaring number of individuals are adopting habits antithetical to thinkingÔÇöoffloading even the smallest of cognitive tasks onto Chat GPT, doomscrolling for hours on social media, and numbing the mind with addictive ÔÇ£brain rot.ÔÇØ Critical thinking is being traded for passive acceptance and the death of curiosityÔÇöall in the name of efficiency and capital innovation. Our minds have become the new commodity.
In response to this, IÔÇÖve seen a clear cultural shift toward self-education. I, too, have been influenced by this new wave of autodidactic learningÔÇöpersonal curricula, digital detoxes, reading challenges, etc. While these are certainly more examples of internet trends worming their way into our algorithms, beneath them lies something deeper: a collective desire to reclaim our own thoughts.
At the same time, access to quality information and educational materials can be hard to find, whether those resources are locked behind the gates of inflated tuition costs, class fees, or online paywalls. For Colossal, accessibility has always been an ongoing commitment. Over the last 15 years and counting, every article, interview, and instructional aid on our site has remained completely free. Our team believes that the ability to learn about and enjoy art must be universally available.
ÔÇ£IÔÇÖve been using Colossal as my go-to source of art on the internet since 2012 when I was in high school. ItÔÇÖs been a pleasure watching it grow and keep up with the breadth and depth of the work being done in the art world.ÔÇØJake F.
For years, members of this community have made us aware of how beneficial Colossal has been in their learning journeys. Educators of all ages have shared that theyÔÇÖve introduced the website to their students, crafting complete lesson plans from our articles and glossary of art terms. I encountered Colossal for the first time in a classroom, and in another life where I taught elementary students art, it remained one of my most reliable tools.
Out of the classroom, learning persists, and weÔÇÖve been elated to hear many stories about how our daily doses of art allow readers to uncover something new everyday, gain more knowledge about contemporary art, and discover impactful stories that bring about change, connection, and hope.
Now more than ever, creative thought must be nurtured, protected, and free from barriers. Our mission to do this can only continue with you. Today, only 1% of Colossal readers financially support the work we do. Like many independent publications, our members are the cornerstone of our existence and ability to continue sharing art with the world.
We encourage you to join the Colossal community. Now is the time to spread creative ideas, revive curiosity, and defend the ability to think and learn imaginatively.
And as we celebrate teacher appreciation this month, weÔÇÖd like to highlight our deeply discounted education membership, available to current students and educators (just shoot as a quick email and weÔÇÖll get you set up). Thank you for reading, sharing, and learning with us.
Gratefully,Jackie AndresOnline editor, Colossal

schema.headline: Creative Thought Is Essential: A Letter from Our Editor

title: In 'Door to Life,' Pacita Abad Evokes Traditional Yemeni Architecture
description: Throughout her illustrious 32-year career, Pacita Abad (1946-2004) traveled to more than 60 countries. Myriad experiences ultimately introduced he
body:
Throughout her illustrious 32-year career, Pacita Abad (1946-2004) traveled to more than 60 countries. Myriad experiences ultimately introduced her to a wide range of techniques, materials, and relationships, shaping the artistÔÇÖs practice over time. Movement provided an enduring source of new ideas and inspiration, and as she put it, ÔÇ£For me, traveling is my art school.ÔÇØ
In the spring of 1998, Abad visited Yemen. At the time, the country was still in recovery following the Yemeni Civil War, which took place four years prior. Grounded in her rigorous political engagement and the instabilities experienced in her native Philippines, Abad reflected on the immutable significance of cultural practices and their value despite periods of upheaval.
ÔÇ£Door made of straw IIIÔÇØ (1998), oil, acrylic, painted and dyed canvas, painted cloth stitched on canvas, 85 x 56 1/4 inches
ÔÇ£Rather than positioning herself within a nameable lineage of artistic influences who moved in┬áconventional gallery spaces,ÔÇØ Tina Kim Gallery notes,┬áÔÇ£Abad instead favored the inheritance of historically anonymous workers in craft, textiles, and┬áthe decorative arts, from locations outside of established Western institutional and market infrastructure.ÔÇØ This quote appears in a statement for the New York galleryÔÇÖs third solo exhibition of AbadÔÇÖs works, titled Door to Life.
The presentation highlights a body of work Abad completed in subsequent years, which takes inspiration from YemenÔÇÖs vibrant, ornate displays of architecture and decoration. One particular focus was doors, which the gallery refers to as ÔÇ£portals.ÔÇØ Through countless photographs and numerous sketches of doorways she encountered on excursions, Abad created a prolific visual archive to take home and use as reference, adding ÔÇ£Everyday a new idea, everyday a new door.ÔÇØ
Installation view of ÔÇ£Pacita Abad: Door to LifeÔÇØ
Crafted in her signature trapunto style, AbadÔÇÖs vertical, rectangular compositions layer meaning, memories, and material. Painted and appliqu├®d geometric patterns on canvas call to decorative elements found in traditional Yemeni architecture, like tessellations and botanical motifs.
The artistÔÇÖs series of never-before-seen qamariya paintings are evocative of the semicircular glass windows common in Sanaa, the nationÔÇÖs capitalÔÇöanother key element of YemenÔÇÖs time-honored artisan practices. The Arabic term qamariya translates to ÔÇ£moon-likeÔÇØ or ÔÇ£of the moon,ÔÇØ echoing the glass structuresÔÇÖ half-moon shape and dynamic ability to transmit light.
Door to Life continues through June 20 at Tina Kim Gallery in New York. You can also flip through more works, which were previously compiled into a small publication that supplemented AbadÔÇÖs initial Door to Life exhibition in 1999.
ÔÇ£Door made of straw IÔÇØ (1998), oil, acrylic, printed cloth, dyed canvas stitched on straw mat, 89 x 53 1/8 inches
Detail of ÔÇ£White Heightens the Awareness of the SensesÔÇØ (1998)
ÔÇ£I Am By The Door in a SecondÔÇØ (1999), oil, painted cotton collaged and stitched on canvas, 61 1/2 x 38 1/8 inches
ÔÇ£White Heightens the Awareness of the SensesÔÇØ (1998), oil, acrylic, oil pastel, dyed cotton, painted canvas, painted cloth stitched on canvas, 84 x 63 inches
Installation view of ÔÇ£Pacita Abad: Door to LifeÔÇØ
ÔÇ£Beside YouÔÇØ (2001), oil and painted canvas stitched on canvas, 18 1/2 x 18 1/2 x 2 inches framed
ÔÇ£Stained glass door in SanaaÔÇØ (1998), oil, printed cloth, painted canvas stitched on canvas 83 x 61 3/4 x 1 1/4 inches
ÔÇ£Rainbow doorÔÇØ (1998), oil, painted printed cloth stitched on canvas, 82 1/4 x 58 1/2 x 1 1/4 inches
ÔÇ£Gray BorderÔÇØ (2001), oil and painted canvas stitched on canvas, 18 1/2 x 18 1/2 x 2 inches framed
ÔÇ£Door Connects Me to the Greatest Happiness I HaveÔÇØ (1999), oil, painted cloth, buttons stitched on padded canvas, 59 3/4 x 38 5/8 inches
Qamariya Window (series) (2000), oil on paper, dimensions variable
Detail of ÔÇ£Door made of straw IIIÔÇØ (1998)
Pacita Abad with ÔÇ£Door to LifeÔÇØ paintings in Jakarta (1999). Courtesy of the Pacita Abad Art Estate
Pacita Abad with ÔÇ£Door to LifeÔÇØ paintings in Jakarta (1999). Courtesy of the Pacita Abad Art Estate
